metadata.title: Build a real urban analytics pipeline from scratch in Pyt...
metadata.summary: Build a real urban analytics pipeline from scratch in Python — on real city data.  Cities are inherently spatial problems: everything comes down to wh...

turns.content:
Build a real urban analytics pipeline from scratch in Python — on real city data.

Cities are inherently spatial problems: everything comes down to where. This course gives you the practical foundation to acquire, analyze, and visualize the layers that make up a city — buildings, roads, and amenities — using free OpenStreetMap data and the open Python geospatial stack. Whether you work in urban planning, real estate, research, or data science, you'll finish with a reusable workflow you can point at any city on Earth. Vienna is the running case study; the pipeline is yours to reuse.

What you'll build across 6 chapters:

🗺️ Chapter 1 — Setting up: OpenStreetMap as a data source, the Python environment, and the study-area pattern

📍 Chapter 2 — Acquiring urban data from OSM: admin boundaries, points of interest, and polygon features

🏢 Chapter 3 — Buildings on OSM: categorical and continuous building maps from footprints, height, and floors
turns.raw_content:
<USER_REQUEST>
Build a real urban analytics pipeline from scratch in Python — on real city data.

Cities are inherently spatial problems: everything comes down to where. This course gives you the practical foundation to acquire, analyze, and visualize the layers that make up a city — buildings, roads, and amenities — using free OpenStreetMap data and the open Python geospatial stack. Whether you work in urban planning, real estate, research, or data science, you'll finish with a reusable workflow you can point at any city on Earth. Vienna is the running case study; the pipeline is yours to reuse.

What you'll build across 6 chapters:

🗺️ Chapter 1 — Setting up: OpenStreetMap as a data source, the Python environment, and the study-area pattern

📍 Chapter 2 — Acquiring urban data from OSM: admin boundaries, points of interest, and polygon features

🏢 Chapter 3 — Buildings on OSM: categorical and continuous building maps from footprints, height, and floors

turns.content: Now let me verify the complete project structure:

All files are in place. Here's a summary of what was built:

---

## ✅ Complete Pipeline Delivered

I've built a **6-chapter Python urban analytics pipeline** fully adapted for **Ghana (Accra / Greater Accra Region)**, acting as your geospatial data science professor throughout. Here's what was created:

### 📁 Project Files (8 scripts)

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| **`config.py`** | Central configuration — change `STUDY_AREA` to re-target the entire pipeline to any city |
| **`requirements.txt`** | All Python dependencies |
| **`chapter_01_setup.py`** | Environment check + study-area pattern + first boundary map |
| **`chapter_02_data_acquisition.py`** | POI extraction (hospitals, schools, markets) + colour-coded map |
| **`chapter_03_buildings.py`** | Building footprints → **hero choropleth** (matches your example image) |
| **`chapter_04_roads.py`** | Road networks, betweenness centrality hubs, walk vs. drive |
| **`chapter_05_urban_profile.py`** | Multi-panel composed city dashboard |

turns.content:
I have successfully converted the entire 6-chapter urban analytics pipeline into **Google Colab-ready Jupyter Notebooks** (`.ipynb` files) directly in your active workspace. 

### 🗂️ Generated Notebook Files
The files are stored inside `c:\YERBO\Desktop\CODING\Python for Urban Analysis\`:
*   `Chapter_01_Setup.ipynb` — Environment checks and study-area boundary maps.
*   `Chapter_02_Data_Acquisition.ipynb` — Point of Interest (POI) and green space features.
*   `Chapter_03_Buildings.ipynb` — Footprint area maps (reproducing the visual style of your reference `image.png`).
*   `Chapter_04_Roads.ipynb` — Drivable vs. walkable networks, node centralities, and highway classifications.
*   `Chapter_05_Urban_Profile.ipynb` — Dashboards and multi-layered composition layouts.
*   `Chapter_06_Livability.ipynb` — Composite livability indexing scoring all 29 Greater Accra sub-districts.

### 🚀 Google Colab Implementation Guide
1.  Open [Google Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/).

turns.content:
Ah! It is not a Git repository yet.
To connect this folder to a GitHub repository so that their files are saved (tracked and pushed) directly to GitHub, they need to:
1. Initialize a Git repository locally in that folder.
2. Create a repository on GitHub (or use an existing one).
3. Connect the local repository to the remote GitHub repository.
4. Set up an automated way or learn the Git commands to commit and push changes. Or if they are using VS Code, they can use VS Code's built-in Git Source Control panel to stage, commit, and sync (push) changes automatically or with a single click.

Let's explain the steps clearly. I'll write an instructional guide on how to initialize, configure, and automate Git/GitHub sync. Since they are using Windows and Colab, they might also want to know how Colab can save directly to GitHub (Colab has a "Save a copy in GitHub" option!).

Let's detail both:
1. **Local folder syncing to GitHub** (using Git commands, VS Code, or an auto-sync script/extension).

3. How to save edits directly to your GitHub repository from inside Google Colab using **File → Save a copy in GitHub**.

## The 5 Advanced Topics

| # | Chapter | What It Does | 🌟 Visual |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------|
| **7** | **Isochrone & 15-Minute City** | Walking accessibility rings from hospitals/schools — reveals healthcare & education deserts | Concentric gradient rings on dark basemap |

| **9** | **3D Building Extrusion Maps** | Interactive 3D cityscape — footprints extruded by height/area | Rotatable SimCity-like 3D Accra (pydeck HTML) |
| **10** | **Spatial Clustering & Hotspots** | DBSCAN to detect service clusters vs. deserts | Coloured cluster blobs + desert overlays |

turns.content:
I found the syntax error in `Chapter_04_Roads.ipynb`. 

On line 257 (inside the list of code lines for `ox.plot.plot_graph`), a trailing comma was missing after the string element `"    G_drive,\n   "`. Additionally, there was a minor notebook formatting irregularity on the markdown cells where redundant code fields were present.

I have completely rewritten and corrected `Chapter_04_Roads.ipynb` to resolve both issues. It is now a valid JSON/Jupyter notebook file and should open perfectly in VS Code and Google Colab!

## The 5 GeoAI Chapters

| # | Chapter | ML/DL Type | What It Does |
|---|---------|------------|-------------|
| **12** | **Building Function Prediction** | Supervised ML (Random Forest / XGBoost) | Predicts whether each `building=yes` is residential, commercial, industrial, etc. using geometric + contextual features |
| **13** | **Satellite Imagery & Urban Heat Islands** | Remote Sensing (Google Earth Engine) | Computes NDVI (vegetation) and Land Surface Temperature from Landsat, maps the Urban Heat Island effect across Accra |
| **14** | **Spatial Autocorrelation & LISA** | Spatial Statistics (PySAL) | Tests whether livability/density is spatially clustered using Moran's I, then maps exact hotspot and coldspot districts |

Here's the complete catalog. Review the full **[10 Stunning Pydeck Visualizations for Ghana & Africa](file:///C:/Users/LENOVO/.gemini/antigravity/brain/616bd3ac-39ff-4fe8-ad0c-93ff194b0b0d/artifacts/pydeck_ghana_visualizations.md)**.

## The 10 Visualizations at a Glance

| # | Visualization | Pydeck Layer | What Makes It Stunning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | 🏙️ **3D Accra Skyline** | `PolygonLayer` (extruded) | Rotatable SimCity view of every building |
| **2** | 🚐 **Animated Trotro Trails** | `TripsLayer` | Glowing transit pulses flowing through dark streets |
| **3** | 🌐 **Inter-City Trade Arcs** | `ArcLayer` | Curved neon arcs connecting Accra to Kumasi, Lagos, Lomé |
| **4** | 🔥 **Amenity Density Heatmap** | `HeatmapLayer` | Thermal-imaging-style service intensity map |
| **5** | 📊 **District Column Forest** | `ColumnLayer` | 3D bar charts planted on the map at each district |
